---
title: "Calibrating ladder fuel contributions to crown fire initiation"
author: 
  D.D.B. Perrakis
  D.K. Thompson
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
format: html
bibliography: references.bib
tbl-cap-location: top
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
df-print: kable
---

### \[Set environment, get data\]

```{r get.data, include=FALSE}
.libPaths("C:/Dan/RPackages")

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, root.dir = "c:/Dan/_Remote_projects/LadderFuels2")

rm(list=ls())
.libPaths("C:/Dan/RPackages")

require(tidyverse)

load(file='./files/mcF.rda')
load(file='./files/wbmc5.rda')
load(file='./files/ISI.rda')

#get fd 
fd4 <- read.csv('./files/fire_data_2026.csv') %>%  
  mutate(CFI2=factor(CFI1, labels=c('Surface', 'Crown')))

#Deciduous data
d1 <- read.csv('./files/fire_data_d1_apr2024.csv')

```

### \[Create DFs, isi.mcsa columns\]

```{r create data.frames, include=FALSE}
#Combine conifer data with D1
fd.all <- bind_rows(fd4 %>% select(
  num:RH, Grass.Cure) %>%
    mutate(Date=ymd(Date)), 
  d1 %>% select(num:RH, Grass.Cure) %>%
    mutate(Date=ymd(Date)))  

#assign project codes - Sharpsand
sharp <- filter(fd4, str_detect(ExpProject, "Sharpsand IM")) %>% pull(num)  #original Sharpsand Immature stands only
sharp.th <- filter(fd4, str_detect(ExpProject, "Sharpsand TH")) %>% pull(num)
sharp.sm <- filter(fd4, str_detect(ExpProject, "Sharpsand SM")) %>% pull(num)

#get sharpsand (IM and TH) stand data
sh.fires0 <- filter(fd4, num %in% c(sharp, sharp.th, sharp.sm)) %>% 
  select(fire, Date, num, ws, MC.SA, MC.SA_dens, MC.SA_season, FMC, 
         FFMC, DMC, ISI, FSG1, SFC, ROS, 
         DC, BUI, FWI, CFI1, CFI2, Site) %>%
  mutate(Exp=case_when(
           num %in% sharp ~'Imm',
           num %in% sharp.th ~'Thin',
           num %in% sharp.sm ~'Semi-M' ),
         Plot=str_extract(fire, '\\d+') %>% as.integer) #extract plot num. from string

sh.fires1 <- mutate(rowwise(sh.fires0), 
                    MC.SA2=wbmc5(ffmc=FFMC, dmc=DMC, stand=4, 
                                 density=2, season=MC.SA_season),
                    MC.SA3=ifelse(FFMC > 92.9, 
                                  wbmc5(ffmc=FFMC, dmc=DMC, stand=4, 
                                 density=2, season=MC.SA_season), 
                                 wbmc5(ffmc=FFMC, dmc=DMC, stand=4,
                                       density=3, season=MC.SA_season))) 
                #MC.SA2 assumes density of 2 for all
                #MC.SA3 assumes dens of 3 for all
                      
#Calculate isi.sa, using mcSA and wind speed:
isi.mcsa <- function(mc, ws) {
  #m = 147.2*(101-ffmc)/(59.5+ffmc)  #original mc from FFMC (mcF) formula
  f_w = exp(0.05039*ws)
  f_f = (91.9*exp(-0.1386*mc))*(1+(mc^5.31)/(4.93*10^7))
  return(0.208*f_w*f_f)
}


#main dataframe for threshold analysis - Sharpsand fires
sh.fires <- mutate(rowwise(sh.fires1), 
                isi.m=isi.mcsa(mc=MC.SA, ws=ws),  #isi.m uses actual MC.SA
                isi.m2=isi.mcsa(mc=MC.SA2, ws=ws),
                isi.m3=isi.mcsa(mc=MC.SA3, ws=ws)) %>% #alternate where all are Dens=2
  as.data.frame()

```

### \[SROS models\]

```{r sros, include=FALSE}
#get models from sROS project; also need associated models saved
#
load('./files/sros-m9.rda')
load('./files/sros-m12.rda')
load('./files/sros-m13.rda')
load('./files/sros-m14.rda')
load('./files/sros-m16.rda')
load('./files/sros-m17.rda')
load('./files/sros-m18.rda')
m20 <- function(ws) {0.2*ws}

#full models
load('./files/m9-mod.rda')
load('./files/m12-mod.rda')
load('./files/m13-mod.rda')
load('./files/m14-mod.rda')
load('./files/m16-mod.rda')
load('./files/m17-mod.rda')
load('./files/m18-mod.rda')

```

### \[VW.lf mods and lf function fc.se\]

```{r vw.lf mods, include=FALSE}
#Vw crown fire model-critical SFC
vw.sfc <- function(fmc=100, z, ros) {
  h = 460+25.9 * fmc
  I_0 = (0.01*h*z)^1.5
  #return(I_0)
  #Byram59
  sfc_0 = I_0/(300*ros)
  return(sfc_0)
  #critical sfc for crowning; needs ROS input
}

#VW model - critical surface intensity for crowning
vwi <- function(fmc=100, z) {
  h = 460+25.9 * fmc
  I_0 = (0.01*h*z)^1.5
  return(I_0)
}

#ladder fuel scaling: surface-equivalent fuel consumption
#changed from lf.sfc
fc.se <- function(zg, cl, fcl) {
  #zg is overstory LCBH
  #cl is ladder fuel height, or midstory fuel centroid (e.g. crown centroid of
  #an understory spruce)
  fc_se=(zg/(zg-cl))^1.5 * fcl
  return(fc_se)
}

#Isn't this wrong?? equation should be fc_se=(zg/cl)^1.5 * fcl
#No - it's correct! Since the key difference is FSG; ignition of ladder fuels
#to LCBH, not ignition of ladder fuels from ground

#save(fc.se, file='c:/Dan/_Fire_tools/rscripts/fc-se.rda')


#Assume Sharpsand FSG ~ 4.5 m
#Snag height ~ 3.2 m
#with crown ratio of 0.5, dead CBH=1.6m, centroid = 3.2-(3.2 * 0.5 * 0.5)= 
zl.test = 4.5-3.2+(3.2 * 0.5 * 0.5)  #FSG_L

```

### \[Calculate FC_L for Sharpsand plots\]

```{r get stand data calculate FC_L}
#Get original sharpsand plot data - density, snags, treatments - 18 plots, 19 fires (p11 twice)
#includes IM (ctrl), TH treatments
sh.full <- read.csv('c:/Dan/_Remote_projects/ccp_git/ccp-cfi/sharp_full.csv') %>%
  mutate(FSG=LCBH) %>%
  select(-c(X, BA, BA.d, LCBH.L)) %>%
  left_join(sh.fires %>% filter(Exp %in% c('Thin', 'Imm')), by='Plot') %>%
  select(-c(fire, Date, num, DC, FWI, CFI2, Exp)) 

#CFL values for dead ladder fuels from Stocks 1987 - IM/ctrl plots only (ones with LF)
lf.cfl <- c(0.142, 0.073, 0.313, 0.183, 0.133, 0.146, 0.131, 0.131,
              0.176, 0.182, 0.151, 0.218, 0.084) #p11 twice for 11a, 11b

#crown fuel consumption estimates by fire type
cfc.p <- c(0.39, 0.49, 0.22, 0.46)
cfc.c <- c(0.89, 1.16, 0.99, 1.27, 1.06, 1.4, 1.04, 1.03, 1.11)
cfc.s <- c(0, 0, 0.08, 0, 0)

#* 0.815# using woody consumption? use full CFL here I think; 
#ICFME surface woody consumption was 0.6 (Stocks et al 2004)
#Also recall ICFME: Stocks et al (2004): 70-86% of overstory 0-1 cm roundwood were consumed, with moisture contents from 55.5-90.4 % 
#down woody fuel moisture contents were 6.3-14.5 %

#Although P7, 18 (ctrl) and P8, 9, 16 (th) are surface fires, assume dead snags burn
dead.cfc.mean <- mean(lf.cfl)

s.ha.ctrl <- filter(sh.full, TRT=='ctrl') %>% 
  pull(s.ha.d) #stems/ha, ctrl stands
dead.cfl.snag <- mean(lf.cfl*10000/s.ha.ctrl)  #mean snag weight cfl (<1 cm) per stem, from ctrl stands; in kg/stem

lf.cflp16 <- round(6875 * dead.cfl.snag / 10000, 3)  #est. lf.cfl for p16 based on 
  #num. of snags, mean cfl (< 1 cm) per snag

sh.full$TRT[17] <- 'ctrl'  #assign partly-thinned one to ctrl grp

lf.cfl2 <- c(0.142, 0.073, 0.313, 0.183, 0.133, 0.146, 0.131, 0.131,
              0.176, 0.182, 0.151, lf.cflp16, 0.218, 0.084) #added p16

#crown ratio equation - from Perrakis(2023) Sup analysis
sharp.cr <- function(dbh) {0.3705 + 0.009196*dbh}

#process LF in control/IM stands
sh.ctrl <- filter(sh.full, TRT=='ctrl') %>% 
  mutate(cfc.d=lf.cfl2, #dead cfc as dead cfl2
         cfc.d.stem=cfc.d * 10000/s.ha.d,   #kg/m2*10000 m2/ha/s/ha = kg/stem
         cen.l= HT.D * 0.75,  #ladder centroid of dead crown consumption; 
         #assume CR of 0.5 and full branch structure; worth testing - 
         #alternative would be HT.D *0.5
         fc.se.ctrl=fc.se(zg=FSG, cl=cen.l, fcl=cfc.d), #calculate surface-equiv.
         #ladder fuel consumption
         fc.se.stem=fc.se.ctrl/s.ha.d,
         fc.se.ctrl2=fc.se(zg=FSG, cl=HT.D/2, fcl=cfc.d)) #alternative fc.se

#simplified df for analysis
sh.ctrl.model <-sh.ctrl %>%
  select(Plot, ISI, isi.m, FSG, SFC, cfc.d, 
         fc.se.ctrl, fc.se.ctrl2, CFI=CFI1) %>%
  mutate(sfc.final=SFC+fc.se.ctrl,
         sfc.final2=SFC+fc.se.ctrl2)

# cfc per dead stem, kg
cfc.dstem <- summarize(sh.ctrl, mean(cfc.d.stem)) %>% pull()

sh.th0 <- filter(sh.full, TRT=='th')

sh.s.ha.d.th <- filter(sh.th0, !Plot %in% c(8,9)) %>%
  summarize(mean(s.ha.d)) 

#Thinned plots, mean dead stems/ha where data was missing
sh.th <- mutate(sh.th0, 
                    s.ha.d=ifelse(
                      Plot %in% c(8, 9), sh.s.ha.d.th, s.ha.d))
                        # fix s.ha.d value where no data; used to be 2600, but that was including p16


#Process LF in TH stands
sh.th <- sh.th0 %>% 
  mutate(cfc.d.stem=cfc.dstem, #use cfc per tree in ctrl plots 
         #to estimate cfc in thinned plots
         cfc.d=cfc.d.stem * s.ha.d / 10000, 
         #kg * s/ha / 10000m^2/ha to give kg/m^2
         cen.l= HT.D * 0.75,  #crown centroid calcs; assume CR of 0.5;
         fc.se.th=fc.se(zg=FSG, cl=cen.l, fcl=cfc.d),
         fc.se.stem=fc.se.th/s.ha.d,  #surf.equivalent FC per stem
         fc.se.th2=fc.se(zg=FSG, cl=HT.D/2, fcl=cfc.d)) #alt fc.se

sh.th.model <- sh.th %>% select(Plot, ISI, isi.m, FSG=FSG1, SFC, fc.se.th, 
                           fc.se.th2, CFI=CFI1) %>% 
  mutate(sfc.final=SFC+fc.se.th,
         sfc.final2=SFC+fc.se.th2)


```

### \[CFI thresholds and data\]

```{r CFI Thresholds for IM TH SM plots}
#Define levels for Sharp trts:
trts <- c('Imm', 'Thin', 'Semi-M')

fires.stat <- select(sh.fires, CFI1, CFI2, FFMC, ws, SFC, 
                     MC.SA, MC.den1=MC.SA_dens, ISI, Exp, isi.m, 
                     isi.m2, isi.m3) %>%
  mutate(ExpF=factor(Exp, levels=trts)) %>%
  ungroup()
#%>%  mutate(CFI2=as.numeric(CFI2))

#CFI modelling
sh.mod <- glm(CFI1 ~ ISI + ExpF, data=fires.stat, family='binomial') #base model, ISI and treatment; both sig. at alpha=0.05

sh.mod2 <- glm(CFI1 ~ isi.m + ExpF, data=fires.stat, family='binomial') #ISI using MC.SA, density 2 for TH, 3 for others. Works! p < 0.05 for all

sh.mod2b <- glm(CFI1 ~ isi.m2 + ExpF, data=fires.stat, family='binomial') 
#isi.m2 has MC.SA_dens=2 for all

sh.mod3 <- glm(CFI1 ~ isi.m3 + ExpF, data=fires.stat, family='binomial')
#dens3 for all except FFMC > 92.9

#Used sh.mod (ISI) for log. regression graph
#Use both for threshold values

#graph with jittered scatter plot
#Isi.seq <- seq(4, 20, by=0.1)

#which model to use? sh.mod with ISI or sh.mod2 with mc.sa or other
use.mod <- sh.mod
use.mod2 <- sh.mod2b
use.mod3 <- sh.mod3

#generate fake data for log. regression graphs
fdata.IM <- data.frame(fISI=seq(3, 20, by=0.1), fExp='Imm') %>%
  mutate(CFI=predict.glm(use.mod, 
                         newdata=list(ISI=fISI, ExpF=fExp), 
                         type='response'))
                         #newdata=list(isi.m=fISI, ExpF=fExp), 
                         #type='response'))
                         
fdata.SM <- data.frame(fISI=seq(3, 20, by=0.1), fExp='Semi-M') %>%
  mutate(CFI=predict.glm(use.mod, 
                         newdata=list(ISI=fISI, ExpF=fExp), 
                         type='response'))
                         #newdata=list(isi.m=fISI, ExpF=fExp), 
                         #type='response'))

fdata.TH <- data.frame(fISI=seq(3, 20, by=0.1), fExp='Thin') %>%
  mutate(CFI=predict.glm(use.mod, 
                         newdata=list(ISI=fISI, ExpF=fExp), 
                         type='response'))
                         #newdata=list(isi.m=fISI, ExpF=fExp), 
                         #ype='response'))

mod.co<-use.mod$coefficients
mod2.co<-use.mod2$coefficients

#solve for p=0.5 in logistical function; 
#p=exp(b0+b1*ISI+b2*ExpSemi-M+b3*ExpThin)
#so 0.5=1/(1+exp(b0+b1*ISI+b2*ExpSemi-M+b3*ExpThin)
#2=1+exp(b0...)
#1=exp()
#0=b0+b1*ISI+b2*ExpSemi-M+b3*ExpThin
#ISI=(b0+b2*ExpSemi-M+b3*ExpThin)/b1

#for isi.m
#0=b0+b1*isi.m+b2*ExpThin+b3*ExpSemi-M+c


# summary(use.mod)  #
# summary(multcomp::glht(use.mod, 
#                         multcomp::mcp(ExpF='Tukey')))  #requires Exp as factor


```

### \[CFI graph\]

```{r Log-CFI graph, define functions}
#get cf thresholds (p=0.5); see prev. code chunk for derivation
#With ISI model:
isi.thresh.im <-  
  -(mod.co[[1]])/mod.co[[2]]  #
  
isi.thresh.semiM <- 
  -(mod.co[[1]] + mod.co[[4]])/mod.co[[2]]

isi.thresh.th <- 
  -(mod.co[[1]] + mod.co[[3]])/mod.co[[2]]

#with isi.m model:
isim.thresh.im <-  
  -(mod2.co[[1]])/mod2.co[[2]]  #
  
isim.thresh.semiM <- 
  -(mod2.co[[1]] + mod2.co[[4]])/mod2.co[[2]]

isim.thresh.th <- 
  -(mod2.co[[1]] + mod2.co[[3]])/mod2.co[[2]]

  
#thresholds with ISI (sh.mod):
#imm: 6.61
#th: 10.31
#semi-M: 8.62

#thresholds with ISI.mcsa (sh.mod2):
#imm: 4.70
#th: 9.19
#semi-M: 8.95

#'working mean':
#'imm': 5.7
#'th': 9.8

#Main ISI-CFI graph with jittered data, for 3 treatments 
sh.isi.log <- 
  ggplot(fires.stat, aes(y=CFI1, x=isi.m))+
  #scale_shape_manual(values=c(23, 22, 21)) +   #pick nice shapes 
  theme_classic() +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c('purple3', 'orange', 'turquoise2'))+
  scale_shape_manual(values=c(21, 23)) + 
  geom_jitter(aes(fill=Exp, colour=Exp, shape=CFI2), 
              size=2.5, width=0, height = 0.02, alpha=0.4) +  
  geom_line(data=fdata.IM, aes(x=fISI, y=CFI), colour='purple3') +
  geom_line(data=fdata.SM, aes(x=fISI, y=CFI), colour='orange') +
  geom_line(data=fdata.TH, aes(x=fISI, y=CFI), colour='turquoise2') +
  labs(x='ISI', y='p(CFO)') +
  annotate('segment', x=isi.thresh.im, xend=isi.thresh.im, #little cross line
           y=0.4, yend=0.6, alpha=0.4, colour='purple3') +
  annotate('segment', x=isi.thresh.th, xend=isi.thresh.th, #cross line 
           y=0.4, yend=0.6, alpha=0.6, colour='turquoise2') 
  
sh.isi.log

```

### \[M calculations\]

```{r m.calculations, include=FALSE}

#t-test to compare SFC between treatments
#t.test(sh.ctrl$SFC, sh.th$SFC)
#p=0.1409

#m formula
#M=SFCx(ROS_th - ROS_im)/(ROS_im x FC_se_im)

#FC_se_im; -7 is to avoid duplicating p11
fc.se.im.mean <- mean(sh.ctrl$fc.se.ctrl[-7])

#solve for M function
m.function <- function(sfc, ros.th, ros.im, fc.se.im) { 
  m=sfc*(ros.th-ros.im)/(ros.im*fc.se.im)
  return(m)
  #solve for m with IM and TH threshold values
}

#model list
#m20%, m12, m16(mc, ws), m18 (FT=con)
#mod.list.isi <- list(m20, m12.conISI, m16.power, m18.agAsy.FT)

#model list, isi.sa models
#m9(SFC), m13, m17(mcsa, ws)
#mod.list.isim <- list(m9.sros.isim2SFC.mod, m13.conSa, m17.power.sa)

#mod.list.all <- list(m20, m12.conISI, m16.power, m18.agAsy.FT,
#                     m9.sros.isim2SFC.mod, m13.conSa, m17.power.sa)

#loop to get ROS estimates
#pred.list <- vector('list', length(mod.list.all))

isi.vals <- c(isi.thresh.im, isi.thresh.th)
isim.vals <-c(isim.thresh.im, isim.thresh.th)
ffmc.val <- mean(sh.full$FFMC)  #90.89
dmc.val <- mean(sh.full$DMC)   #42
ws.vals <- c(6.055, 14.881)  #ws vals at FFMC 90.89 to get thresh ISI
sfc.mean <- mean(sh.full$SFC)  #1.3374
mcsa.val <- mean(sh.full$MC.SA2) #10.329
ws.vals.sa <- c(5.493, 13.883)  #ws vals at mc.sa 10.329 to get thresh isim

#Model predictions at crown fire thresholds: ISI models
#maybe just use the ones with ISI & mc/ws?
Mm20 <- m20(ws.vals) #ok with FFMC 90.89
Mm12 <- fun.m12(isi.vals) #ok ISI
Mm16 <- fun.m16(ws=ws.vals, mc=mcF(ffmc.val)) #maybe
Mm18 <- fun.m18(isi.vals) #ok FT

#Variable types
vars <- c('ROS: WS', 'ROS: ISI', 'ROS: WS, FFMC', 'ROS: ISI, FT')

#nice simple table of ROS, ISI models
modelMms <- rbind(Mm20, Mm12, Mm16, Mm18) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(Variable=vars)

colnames(modelMms) <- c('ROS.im', 'ROS.th', 'Method')

#Model predictions at CF thresholds: ISI.m models
#If needed, ISI.sa & mc.sa models
Mm9 <- fun.m9(isim=isim.vals, sfc=c(sfc.mean, sfc.mean))
Mm13 <- fun.m13(isi.m=isim.vals)
Mm17 <- fun.m17(ws=ws.vals.sa, mcsa=mcsa.val)

#Var types
vars2 <- c('ROS: ISI_sa, SFC', 'ROS: ISI_sa', 'ROS: WS, mc_sa')

modelMmsa <- rbind(Mm9, Mm13, Mm17) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(Var=vars2)

colnames(modelMmsa) <- c('ROS.im', 'ROS.th', 'Method')

solve.for.m1 <- mutate(modelMms, 
                        M=m.function(sfc=1.337, ros.th=ROS.th,
                              ros.im=ROS.im, fc.se.im=fc.se.im.mean))
#works!
#Mean of 5.23

solve.for.m2 <- mutate(modelMmsa, 
                        M=m.function(sfc=1.337, ros.th=ROS.th,
                              ros.im=ROS.im, fc.se.im=fc.se.im.mean))

m.table0 <- full_join(solve.for.m1, solve.for.m2) %>%
  select(Method, everything()) %>%
  tibble()

#Another way is with M9, using SFC
#TH threshold ROS is reached using IM isim with SFC of 5.604
#this is mean SFC + FC.se*M
#M=(5.604-1.337)/FC.se
#M=5.780317
Mm9.sfc <- (5.064-sfc.mean)/fc.se.im.mean  #5.7803

expert1 <- 8.62818
expert2 <- 6.73126

solve.expert <- data.frame(Method=c('Expert 1', 'Expert 2'), 
                           ROS.im=NA, ROS.th=NA, M=c(expert1, expert2))

solve.m9 <- data.frame(Method='ROS & SFC', ROS.im=NA, ROS.th=NA, M=Mm9.sfc)

#Table with all M estimates
m.table <- bind_rows(solve.expert, solve.m9, m.table0)

grand.mean.M <- mean(c(solve.for.m1 %>% pull(M), solve.for.m2 %>% pull(M),
                  Mm9.sfc, expert1, expert2) )
#5.561
#To here Apr. 23
#Should also make same calcs for CR=1


#another tactic would be to make the case for NO M! (M=1)

m.table

```
##\[Empirical crown fire reanalysis]
```{r}
xv.sum2 <-read.csv('C:/Dan/_Remote_projects/ccp_git/ccp-cfi/tables/xv_sum2_2024.csv')

xv.sum2 %>% select(-1, -3) %>% as.data.frame()
```



## Abstract

Predicting the onset of conifer crown fires is an uncertain process.
Ladder fuels (LF), biomass that act as a bridge between surface and
canopy fuel layers, are suspected of being disproportionately important
in crown fire initiation, but are difficult to quantify and incorporate
into prediction systems. A somewhat similar problem occurs with
multi-story canopy fuels, whereby ignition of intermediate-height layers
is first required in order for higher canopy elements to become
involved. In this study, we show how a simple rearranging of C.E. Van
Wagner's classic model (Can. J. For Res. 1977, v7: 23-34) reframes crown
fire initiation as a critical fuel consumption threshold-driven process.
A new equation is presented for comparing fuel consumption at different
vertical positions, which allows a rescaling of LF consumption down to
the ground level as an additive element to actual surface fuels. To test
the theoretical model, we used several sources of evidence, primarily
from Canadian experimental fires. Incorporating LF into empirical crown
fire models improved model predictions, and best results were obtained
with a LF multiplier of approximately 4. This suggests that LF
consumption is about four times as influential per unit mass as conifer
surface fuel consumption, even after accounting for vertical position.
The resulting models will be useful for improve fire behaviour
predictions as well as for improving the design of hazard reduction
treatments.

## 1. Introduction

Conifer forests cover most of the circumboreal region of the world and
crown fires represent a significant fire-related hazard in these stands
[@kneeshaw2011; @stocks1991]. As noted by Kiil et al. [-@kiil1976] and
quoted by Cruz et al. [-@cruz2005], surface fires rarely spread \> 6
m/min under closed conifer stands, as higher spread rates are usually
associated with some degree of crowning or torching. Once flames reach
the upper reaches of the canopy, they can be directly influenced by open
air winds in the lower atmosphere. The fully developed crowning process
has long been known to be associated with a dramatic increase in fire
spread rate, due to several processes including flame tilt, increased
lee-side wind exposure and vigorous ember spotting [@taylor2004;
@alexander2016].

Stand structure is well-known to influence crown fire occurrence and
tendency. While crown fires occur readily in short trees (\< \~12 m
height) of the northern boreal forest, the element in fuel complexes
that enables crown fires in most tall conifer stands are ladder fuels
(LF). This term refers to any easily-ignited fuels in the mid-canopy
space, meaning fine-textured and flammable dead or live biomass above
the surface fuel complex but below the live canopy base. In Canada,
early wildfire researcher J.G. Wright first noted nearly a century ago
that 'crown fires are not likely to occur unless there is a large volume
of fuel under the trees' [@wright1932]. Van Wagner [@vanwagner1977a], in
describing his classic crown fire initiation (CFI) model based on
empirical data and physical convection theory, noted that such 'bridge
fuels' consisted of 'combustible matter such as loose bark, dead lower
branches, lichen, small conifers, etc.' located above the surface fuel
complex, remarking that such fuels needed to be 'present in sufficient
quantity to intensify the surface fire appreciably as well as to extend
the flame height'. While the publication describing Van Wagner's model
has become a classic (\> 1600 citations as of early 2026), practical
methods for quantifying the LF influence are still lacking nearly a
half–century later.

Van Wagner's model was originally developed to identify the critical
surface fire intensity ($I_0$) to enable CFI:

$$
I_0=(chz)^{1.5}
$$

\[eq. 1\], where $h$ is heat of ignition, $z$ is live crown base height
(LCBH), and $c$ is a dimensional constant, derived empirically; as
Alexander and Cruz [@alexander2016] have noted, the $c$ value is
somewhat questionable and may be stand-dependent. Most modelling systems
have combined $I_0$ with Byram's [-@byram1959] fireline intensity
equation in order to solve for critical rate of spread. This combination
has allowed for linkages with Rothermel's [-@rothermel1972]
semi-physical surface rate of spread model [e.g. @scott2001;
@andrews2014], or with empirical rate of spread (ROS) functions, such as
in the FBP System [@forestrycanadafiredangergroup1992]. Surprisingly, a
simple algebraic rearranging of the terms of Van Wagner's model allows
for a useful and powerful estimator applicable to LF elements at varying
heights above ground. Empirical data presently allows for calibration of
the theoretical and conceptual linkages between surface and crown fire
processes.

### 1.1 Objective

The objectives of this study were to 1) present a theoretical framework
for analyzing LF influence in crown fire initiation, as previously
described [@perrakis2025]; 2) calibrate and evaluate the theoretical
equation with field data; and 3) incorporate the LF function into an
empirical operational crown fire model for use with estimated fuel
structure variables.

### 1.2 Theoretical model: scaling ladder fuel consumption to the surface

Ladder fuels are defined by their fine texture and intermediate vertical
position [@vanwagner1977a; @kilgore1975]. LF exist in conifer stands
between the surface fuels, at $z=0$, and canopy fuels, which for our
purposes begin at the live canopy base height (LCBH). When continuous
with the surface fuelbed and ignited by a proximate source (i.e. an
advancing fireline), the heat flux contribution of these fuels
[@melnik2022] is much closer to the canopy base, and therefore much more
likely to contribute to canopy ignition compared to similar fuel
quantities at the surface.

It was previously shown [@perrakis2025] that the formulation of the Van
Wagner [-@vanwagner1977a] crown initiation model enables an algebraic
equivalency between fuel consumption and the inverse of *z* (LCBH). The
equations will be presently reviewed for clarity. Replacing $I_0$
(eq. 1) with Byram's [-@byram1959] fireline intensity formula produces
the following popular transformation [e.g., @fcfdg1992; @scott2001]:

$$
H \cdot SFC \cdot ROS=(c \cdot h)^{1.5} z^{1.5}  
$$

\[eq. 2\], where $H$ is the heat of combustion, $SFC$ is surface fuel
consumption, and $ROS$ is rate of forward spread. In addition to solving
for critical ROS for crown fire initiation [@vanwagner1977a;
@Alexander1988a], this can also be solved for critical SFC:

$$
SFC_0=\frac {(ch)^{1.5} z^{1.5}} {h \cdot ROS} 
$$

\[eq. 3\]. Different LCBH levels, $z_1$ and $z_2$, can also be compared,
holding all other variables equal:

$$
\frac {SFC_2}{SFC_1}=
\frac {  \left[  \frac {(ch)^{1.5} z_2^{1.5}} {h \cdot ROS} \right] } 
{  \left[  \frac {(ch)^{1.5} z_1^{1.5}} {h \cdot ROS} \right] }
$$

\[eq. 4\], assuming no change in ROS with varying LCBH. This simplifies
to a more basic relationship between fuel consumption at different
heights beneath a flammable canopy:

$$
SFC_2=(\frac {z_2} {z_1})^{1.5} \cdot SFC_1
$$

\[eq. 5\]. In the case of the consumption of an elevated LF quantity
$FC_L$, positioned at a certain height above ground $C_L$ (ladder fuel
centroid) in the forest, the surface-equivalent $FC$ value ($FC_{SE}$)
is defined using a modification of eq. 5, where the influence of $FC_L$
represents an inflated consumption quantity due to its elevated position
and therefore disproportional influence on crown fire. In this case, $z$
remains the normal LCBH for the live canopy and $z-C_L$ represents the
fuel strata gap between LF elements and the LCBH, the key vertical gap
in this situation for representing the LF influence:

$$
FC_{SE}=(\frac {z} {z-C_L})^{1.5} \cdot FC_L ~~, ~~~~~~ z > C_L
$$

\[eq. 6\]. Using Equation 6, it becomes possible to estimate the
contribution of canopy fuel elements and compare them with the surface
fuel complex in an additive manner.

There are several concerns with this relationship that must be addressed
before it can be inserted into a crown fire initiation or occurrence
model. The first is that an adequate estimate of $FC_L$ must be
available, likely from a previous study or model. The second issue is
that eq. 6 assumes that LF and surface fuel structure and combustion
properties are identical, which is seldom the case. Surface fuels in
forested systems tend to be much more compact (higher bulk density and
packing ratio) than crown fuel complexes, including many LF types, with
a much more aeration-limited combustion environment [@rothermel1972;
@schwilk2015; @keane2015]. The general FBP System approach to SFC, where
all consumed surface and ground fuels are assumed to contribute to
surface fire intensity [@vanwagner1977a;
@forestrycanadafiredangergroup1992], may overemphasize dense duff fuels
that burn in post-frontal combustion and ignores factors such as
residence time[@nelson1988; @wotton2012]. In contrast, fine canopy and
ladder fuel strata are much more aerated (more porous) but also
vertically and horizontally discontinuous. These differences could be
addressed theoretically or, as in the present investigation,
empirically.

Thirdly, while equation 6 specifies that the ladder fuel height cannot
exceed the LCBH, it is apparent that as the difference between them
($z-C_L$) diminishes, $FC_G$ can grow to extreme levels, eventually
producing a division by zero problem at $z=C_L$. Thus, 0.1
$kg \cdot m^{-2}$ of fuel consumption 1 m below a 5 m tall canopy base
($z-C_L=1$) scales to equate to ground-level SFC of 1.12 kg m^-2^ , but
the same fuel consumption 0.2 m below the same canopy scales to
$FC_G$=12.5 kg m^-2^. This logic holds in a theoretical sense – a fire
is much more likely to ignite the lower live canopy if the burning fuel
is immediately beneath, and a 0.2 m gap is almost negligible at the fuel
particle scale. However, this suggests that caution is needed to utilize
equation 6 in a practical sense to avoid inflating the ladder fuel
effect to illogical levels.

### 1.3 Ladder fuel multiplier

One simple approach to the challenge of comparing high porosity ladder
fuels with low porosity surface fuels is to evaluate the use of a
multiplier. This would be, in the simplest sense, a value that could be
used to rescale the value of $FC_L$ in order to bring it in line with
SFC values. We thefore define a rescaled value for LF consumption that
allows $FC_L$ to be comparable (in an additive sense) with consumed
quantities of compact forest floor and woody fuel material, after
rescaling using eq. 6. The new equation to be tested is
$SFC=FC_{SE} \cdot M$\[eq. 7\], with $M$ being the appropriate
multiplier, derived empirically.

Measured and estimated SFC values in Canadian experimental fires ranged
from 0.2 to 3.8 kg m^-2^ [@perrakis2023]. The value of this multiplier,
$M$, could range from 1 (no effect) to 10 or more in the event that
ladder fuel biomass and arrangement appeared to have more than an order
of magnitude greater effect than surface biomass on vertical heat flux
and crown fire initiation.

The remainder of the study tests and discusses empirical derivation of
*M* and further validation using previously documented Canadian
experimental fire data.

## 2. Methods

Three separate validation methods were used to evaluate the theoretical
ladder fuel scaling relationship and calibrate the value of the
multiplier M: 1) an expert opinion assessment, as well as a crown fire
threshold model between three treatment types, using 2) an SFC-based ROS
model as well as 3) additional models calibrated using Byram's intensity
equation. The inclusion of ladder fuels as well as the estimated *M*
value were further evaluated using a sensitivity analysis of a refitted
crown fire occurrence model. The final step leads to a new concept of
crown fire, where flaming transitions between canopy fuel strata can be
tested and defined individually. All of these relate to stand level
estimates of fuel structure using mean measured values. These techniques
are relatively simple and operationally practical, but may mask
significant impacts of within-stand heterogeneity and spatial pattern of
fuel arrangement (e.g. clumping, proximity to overstory trees, etc.).

### 2.1 Study area: Sharpsand Creek, Ontario

The validation made use of data from the fire experiments at Sharpsand
Creek, Ontario. This site and its observations have been analyzed in
several studies but remain useful for the present purposes. As
previously described, 27 plots in three separate treatment types were
burned between 1974 and 1991 at this site north of Thessalon, Ontario.
The original unaltered stand (immature: IM) consisted of naturally
regenerated jack pine following a 1948 wildfire, with dense stocking of
a single live cohort along with abundant standing dead stems forming
volatile ladder fuels [@walker1975; @stocks1973]. Results from the
initial experimental fires (1975-1981) were published by Stocks
[-@stocks1987] and formed the empirical basis for much of the FBP System
C-4 fuel type [@forestrycanadafiredangergroup1992]. These observations
were also analyzed separately by Van Wagner [@vanwagner1993] and used in
additional fire behaviour studies [@call1997; @albini1986; @cruz2003].
During the course of the early experiments, it was discovered that 6 of
the plots had been hand-thinned (TH) in 1960, removing most of the
regenerating stems and resulting in much less dense stands compared with
the 'control' IM plots [@stocks1987; B. J. Stocks, personal
communication, Jan. 2020].

In the later fires (1988-1991), stand characteristics indicated
significant structural changes underway during the intervening years.
The dense IM plots were initially surveyed at \>9000 live stems ha^-1^
and \> 10,000 dead stems ha^-1^ [measured in 1973; @stocks1987]; when
re-surveyed over a decade later, self-thinning in so-called semi-mature
(SM) stands had reduced live and dead stem density by 53 % and 63 %,
respectively, while total basal area increased by 37 %; average LCBH had
also increased to 5.3 m, about 1 m higher than IM stands [@mcrae2017].

As discussed in a previous study [@perrakis2023], earlier studies
handled these three stand types in various ways. With the IM stands
included in the C-4 fuel type, the TH plots had been deemed more
representative of mature pine stands and previously included in the FBP
System C-3 fuel type; this despite similar age, LCBH and site
characteristics as the IM stands [@degroot2022]. In later studies, the
ladder fuel influence in IM stands was estimated by some authors to act
similarly to a reduction of the LCBH; this was based on the logic of
reduced distance between surface fuel and continuous canopy fuel caused
by the standing dead ladder fuels. This modified LCBH measure was termed
the Fuel Strata Gap, equal to 2 m [@cruz2004; @cruz2006; @perrakis2023].
The raw Sharpsand Creek fire data can be found in the supplemental
material.

### 2.2 Crowning behaviour and ladder fuel consumption

\[Likely move this pgph to Supplemental Material\] There is a question
of which plots experienced crown fire vs surface fire or transitional
torching behaviour. In the original Sharpsand experiments (1974-1981; IM
and TH stands: Stocks 1987; Cruz 1999), fire type was identified as
'surface' or 'crown'. In the SM experiments, however, fire type was
identified as 'surface', 'some torching', 'torching', and 'crown'
[@mcrae2017]. Descriptions in the latter text make it clear that
'torching' fires experienced behaviour akin to passive crowning, while
those described as 'some torching' appeared to be near the crowning
threshold (i.e., primarily surface fires but with \~5-15% crown fraction
burned). Following discussions with experimental leaders (BJ Stocks,
pers. comm. 2020), the two fires described as 'some torching' were
assumed to defined the crowning threshold: fire #16 (ISI=8.1, ROS=3.5
m/min) was considered to be a surface fire observation, while fire #18
(ISI=9.4, ROS=5.1 m/min) was considered a marginal passive crown
fire.\[end move\]

The most challenging value for the analysis is the fuel consumption of
ladder fuels, as this is seldom measured (though sometimes estimated
indirectly) in experimental studies. Stocks [-@stocks1987] presented
estimated dead roundwood fuel consumption for the original Sharpsand
Creek experimental fires, and these values are used in the present
analysis.

For the present calculations, ladder fuel influence was quantified using
equation 6 to scale the estimated ladder fuel consumption (dead canopy
fuel \< 1 cm; Stocks 1987) into an equivalent SFC contribution value,
$SFC_L$. The vertical position of fine standing ladder fuels was assumed
to be the crown centroid of the mean dead snag canopy layer at the stand
level:

$$C_L=CBH_{sap}+\frac {height_{sap}-CBH_{sap}}{2}$$\[eq. 7\]
[@perrakis2023]; $CBH_{sap}$ and $height_{sap}$ are the mean crown base
height and mean height of the dead saplings, respectively. However,
since $CBH_{sap}$ was unknown in our Sharpsand calibration dataset, we
first assumed a crown ratio (CR) of 0.5 [@holdaway1986] and then tested
its sensitivity by a second test with a CR of 1.

![]('./FSG-snags-crown.png')

*Diagram (not to scale) showing approximate estimation of live crown
base height (z), dead ladder fuel centroid (C~L~), and fuel strata gap
(z~L~) for immature jack pine stands (unthinned controls) at Sharpsand
Creek.*

#### Estimation 1 - Expert opinion, Sharpsand Creek (ON)

The first calibration method involved expert opinion from previous
studies. As noted above, Cruz et al. [-@cruz2004] estimated that the
abundant ladder fuels characterizing the Sharpsand Creek stands, in the
form of standing dead trees (including dead branches, bark flakes, and
fine stemwood) amounted to a 'fuel strata gap' of 2 m, compared to
approximately 4 m for the actual LCBH (*z*). We used those values as a
first estimate along with equation 5 to investigate a potential value
for multiplier M.

#### Estimations 2 and 3 - Analyis of crowning thresholds, Sharpsand Creek (ON)

For the second and third estimation methods, we used the Sharpsand
Creek, Ontario experiments once again, including the full complement of
immature (IM), thinned (TH), and semi-mature (SM) treatments in our
analysis [@stocks1987; @mcrae2017; @perrakis2023]. This site, with many
fires in stands that differed primarily by age (time since wildfire) and
treatment, provided the ideal test case for assessing threshold crown
fire conditions and ladder fuel effects. We used logistic regression to
identify the crown fire thresholds between weather indices and
treatments. This was initially done using the initial spread index (ISI)
from the FWI System [@vanwagner1987]; we also tested for thresholds
using a stand-adjusted version of the ISI ($ISI_{sa}$), which was simply
the ISI equation's f(F) function fitted to Wotton and Beverly's
[@wotton2007] stand-adjusted moisture content ($mc_{sa}$)
[@perrakis2026].

We then evaluated the crowning thresholds in terms of critical FC to
solve for the multiplier effect (*M*) using two different approaches.
Method 2 involved using an SFC-based ROS model and the different SFC
values required under identical weather conditions (ISIsa) to equalize
ROS, based on eq. 7. Method 3 used Byram's [-@byram1959] fireline
intensity along with predicted ROS using fitted surface ROS models, and
solving for M using the threshold values.

#### Evaluation using a crown fire model

For the third and final method of examining the theoretical
relationships, we returned to a recent crown fire analysis and attemped
to refit the relationship between fire environment variables and crown
fire occurrence. As in Perrakis et al. [-@perrakis2023], we used 10 m
open wind speed, estimated litter and fine dead surface fuel moisture
(as represented by either the FFMC or the stand-adjusted moisture
content, $mc_{sa}$). This time, rather than subjectively estimating the
FSG adjustments to LCBH in certain stands, we used eq. 7 to estimate the
ladder fuel contributions of standing dead fuels, based on the measured
or estimated \< 1 cm elevated roundwood (dead) consumption values. We
tested only the best-performing model forms for this purpose, which had
log-transformed SFC values and a 3/2 exponent for the LCBH term. The
multiplier *M* in this case was held inside the log-transformed SFC
term; thus, the respective coefficient was fitted to
$log(SFC+FC_{SE} \cdot M)$. The initial value of M in this fitted model
was taken from the previous estimates, but a sensitivity analysis step
also tested several other values of M in addition to the mean calibrated
value.

## 3. Results

#### 3.1 Estimating *M* multiplier - Method 1, Expert opinion

The first estimation method for the multiplier M was based on solving
eq. 5 using the expert opinion values given in Cruz et al. [@cruz2004;
@cruz2005] to account for ladder fuels.

From eq. 5, we can easily rearrange as follows, assuming $SFC_1=SFC_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac {SFC_2} {SFC_1+FC_L \cdot M} &= (\frac {z_2} {z_1})^{1.5} \\
M&= \frac {SFC \cdot (z_1-z_2)^{1.5}} {FC_L \cdot z_2^{1.5}} \\
\end{aligned}$$

\[eq. 8\]. Starting with the approximate LCBH values originally reported
for the Sharpsand IM stands (*z~1~*= 4 m), this value was reduced by 2 m
to produce the FSG estimate (*z~2~*=2 m). The mean estimated *FC~L~* of
the IM plots was 0.155 kg m[^1], and mean *SFC* was 1.337. Therefore, we
solve eq. 8 for our first *M* estimate:

[^1]: -2

$$\begin{aligned}
M
&=\frac {1.337 \cdot (4-2)^{1.5}} {0.155 \cdot 2^{1.5}} \\
M&=8.63 \end{aligned}$$ \[7\]. The same calculation was also completed
with more precise values derived from original stand data [@walker1975].
Detailed plot-level analyses [@degroot2022; @perrakis2023] produced mean
treatment-level LCBH values of 4.36 m in the IM plots. Solving eq. 8
with $z_1=4.36$ and $z_2=2.36$ (following the assumption of
$FSG=z_2=z_1-2$) produces a slightly lower estimate of $M=6.731$.

#### 3.2 Crowning thresholds and critical ROS, Sharpsand Creek

The second multiplier estimation method involved the logistic regression
between treatments. The full dataset consisted of 27 experimental fires:
14 IM, 5 TH, and 8 SM treatments. Both tests revealed *ISI* and
*ISI~sa~* were showed both ISI/ISI_sa and treatment as significant
predictors (⍺=0.05) of crown fire occurrence.

##### 3.2.1 Critical ROS for crowning

Crowning thresholds were `r round(isi.thresh.im, 2)` and
`r round(isi.thresh.th, 2)` ISI for IM (control) and TH (thinned)
stands, respectively. In terms of ISI~SA~, the MC~SA~-based spread
index, the respective thresholds were `r round(isim.thresh.im, 2)` and
`r round(isim.thresh.th, 2)` for IM and TH stands.

Solving for M also required a surface ROS estimate associated with the
crowning thresholds in both stand types. We used an ensemble of 5
different recommended sROS models for hemi-boreal stands, using the ISI,
ISI_sa, or individual moisture and wind speed variables [@perrakis2026]:
M12 (ISI), M13 (ISI_sa), M16, M17, M18 and M20%, the latter being the
'mental arithmetic' model that estimates sROS in conifer stands as 20 %
of the open wind speed. While these sROS models suffer somewhat from
sparse observations for ISI \> \~12, typical crown fire conditions, they
are quite credible under conditions approximating our crowning
thresholds of ISI/ ISI_sa of \~5-10. Approximate surface fire spread
rates of 0.7 – 2.5 m min^-1^ are expected.

##### 3.2.2 *M* Method 2 - SFC-based ROS model

A second method involved using Perrakis et al.'s [-@perrakis2026] ROS
Model 9, which uses the *ISI~sa~* and *SFC* as inputs. The reasoning is
that the difference between the *ISI~sa~* ROS thresholds for IM and TH
stands could be explained by the transformed SFC term.

[note: TH threshold ROS is reached using IM isim with SFC of 5.604
this is mean SFC + FC.se\*M #M=(5.604-1.337)/FC.se ]

##### 3.2.3 *M* Method 3 - Byram's intensity and solving for *M*

We can now use our scaled ladder fuel equation (eq.6) along with
estimated threshold surface ROS and Byram's fire intensity equation
[@byram1959] once again in order to calculate the most likely value for
$M$. This calculation assumes that equivalent critical surface intensity
values ($I_0$) are required for crowning in the control and thinned
stands, both with LCBH of about 4.4 m.

In terms of ROS values at the crowning threshold ($ROS_0$) in the Im and
Th stands, the equivalency is expressed as follows:

$$\begin{align*}
I_{0 \cdot Im} &= I_{0 \cdot Th} \\
h \cdot w_{Im} \cdot ROS_{Im} &= h \cdot w_{Th} \cdot ROS_{Th} \\
SFC_{Im} \cdot ROS_{0 \cdot Im} &= SFC_{Th} \cdot ROS_{0 \cdot Th} \\
(SFC+FC_{SE \cdot Im} \cdot M) \cdot ROS_{0 \cdot Im} &= SFC_{Th} \cdot ROS_{0 \cdot Th} \\
\end{align*}
$$

Based on eq. 6, $FC_{SE}$ is calculated using ladder fuel structure and
estimated consumption of \< 1 cm dead roundwood. In this case, we
calculate ladder fuel height ($C_L$) using an estimated crown ratio of
0.5 and cohort heights from Walker and Stocks' [-@walker1975] original
stand data. Mean dead sapling height and \< 1 cm dead consumption
($FC_L$ ) in control stands were 3.49 m and 0.159 kg m^-2^, resulting in
mean $C_L=2.62$ m. $FC_{SE}$ values (eq. 6), ranged from 0.291 to 1.227
kg m^-2^, with a mean of 0.658 kg m^-2^ .

The final solution was calculated by solving for M:

$$\begin{align*}
(SFC_{Im}+FC_{SE \cdot Im} \cdot M) \cdot ROS_{0 \cdot Im} &= SFC_{Th} \cdot ROS_{0 \cdot Th} \\
M &= \frac {SFC_{Th} \cdot ROS_{0 \cdot Th} - {SFC_{Im} \cdot ROS_{0 \cdot Im}}} {ROS_{Im} \cdot FC_{SE \cdot Im}} \\
\end{align*}
$$ \[eq. 9\], or, if we assume that $SFC_{Th}=SFC_{Im}$,
$$ M=\frac {SFC(ROS_{Th}-ROS_{Im})} {ROS_{Im} \cdot FC_{SE \cdot Im}}$$
\[eq. 10\]. Mean $SFC_{Im}$ was 1.414 kg m\^{-2}, while mean $SFC_{Th}$
was 1.124 kg m\^{-2}, with the difference being non-significant (t-test:
P=0.141); because of the small TH sample size (5 plots), however, it is
an open question whether thinned plots actually had lower consumption.
Using the overall mean SFC of 1.337 kg m\^{-2}, the two spread rate
models produced $$\begin{align*} 
M_{ISI} &= \frac {1.337 (2.862-0.746)} {0.746 \cdot 0.658}  \\
&= 5.770 \\
\end{align*}
$$

using the ISI-based sROS model M12 and crowning thresholds, and
$$\begin{align*}
M_{ISI_{sa}} &= \frac {1.337(1.876-0.286)} {0.286 \cdot 0.658}\\
&= 11.280\\
\end{align*}
$$

using the ISI~SA~-based sROS model M13 and the two threshold ISI~SA~
values.

In addition varying sROS models, the sensitivity analysis also tested
crown ratio (CR) by using the the dead ladder fuel midpoint (cohort
height/2), the equivalent of a crown ratio of 1. This results in
significantly lower FC~SE~ values, due to the greater z~L~ values. The
varied sROS and CR models resulted in the matrix of M estimates shown in
Table X.

Table x. Calculated estimates for *M*, the ladder fuel multiplier 1st
row: ROS thresholds rows: m20%, then isi.mods, then isim.mods columns:
(CR=0.5) IM thresh, TH thresh; (CR=1) IM thresh, TH thresh

ROS, Other models: M18: IM: 1.199803 TH: 2.643482 M=2.445

M20%: Use mean overall FFMC of 90.89 - ws=6.07 (IM), ws=14.88 IM: 1.214
TH: 2.976 M=2.949

m16 (mc, ws): mean FFMC of 90.89 IM: 0.5525988 TH: 3.074282 M=9.272271

m17 (mcsa, ws): mean FFMC of 90.89, mean DMC 42, IM_dens=3; TH_dens=2
IM: 0.4756495 TH: 2.655416 M=9.3117

#### Empirical crown fire analysis

Finally, we reanalyzed the experimental crown fire dataset using Monte
Carlo cross-validation, comparing models with $log(SFC)$,
$log(SFC + FC_{SE})$, and $log(SFC + FC_{SE} \cdot M)$. The starting
value for M was 4, a rough mean of the previous estimates.

As show in the table, the cross-validation evidence supports the
existence of M \> 1. Both the FFMC- and MC~SA~-based models (7, 8) using
M had higher mean accuracy and lower AIC than comparable models
incorporating ladder fuels without a multiplier effect (5, 6). The best
models were superior than either the base (LCBH only; 1, 2) or
subjectively-assigned FSG (3, 4) models.

Table


#### Upper canopy crowning

```{r}
fd.bimodal <- filter(fd7, num %in% c(icfme, kenshoe)) %>%
  filter(!is.na(CFI2)) %>%  #only those that crowned in Spruce
  select(fire, ws, FSG=FSG2, MC.SA, SFC, SFC3, MC.FFMC, CFI2, Site) %>% 
  mutate(pred.m15=predict(model15, newdata=list(
    ws=ws, MC.FFMC=MC.FFMC, SFC3=SFC3, FSG=FSG), 
    type='response'),
         Corr15=round(pred.m15, 0)==CFI2) %>%
  mutate(pred.m16=predict(model16, newdata=list(
    ws=ws, MC.SA=MC.SA, SFC3=SFC3, FSG=FSG), 
    type='response'),
         Corr16=round(pred.m16)==CFI2)

```

## Discussion

Aside from Van Wagner's equation, this is simply a slightly different
representation of Thomas' [-@thomas1963] equation for flame size under
calm conditions.

\- Figure showing more complex architecture; 2-story stands, using Eq 2
to estimate contribution of surface fuels to upper stratum crowning -
Validation 4. Upper canopy crowning -Scale issues are obvious here.
Following the logic and intent of Van Wagner's (1977) model, the scaling
equation represents relationships between canopy strata logically in
1-dimensional space, assuming ladder fuels are positioned beneath
overstory canopy fuels. They also may hold at the single tree level,
where individual ladder fuel elements (saplings, bark flakes, arboreal
lichen) are horizontally juxtaposed with overstory fuels. More detailed
survey methods (e.g. Alexander et al. 2004; Reinhardt et al. 2006; Arkin
et al. 2023) can describe the frequency distributions associated with
vertical strata in a stand, while ecologists have described the spatial
patterns of overstory trees in stands across various stand types (Kenkel
et al. 1997; Larson and Churchill 2012). Future investigations will be
needed on how these spatial distribution patterns affect ladder fuel
influences near crown fire initiation thresholds.

-For the theoretical relationship to hold for actual ladder fuel
elements (flammable shrubs, saplings, bark flakes, hanging lichens,
etc.), it is imperative that they be connected to surface fuels, and in
actual forest settings this is uncertain. Factors such as flame
residence time (refs#) are probably important for determining the degree
of consumption of standing ladder fuels such as dead saplings.

-The concept of a multiplier for comparing elevated fuels to surface
fuels is a similar topic to Van Wagner's \[-vanwagner1977\] 'c'
parameter of 0.01, deduced from a comparison of only two experimental
fires, as discussed by Alexander [@alexander1998]. Densely-packed litter
fuelbeds have lower spread rate than lower density fuelbeds with the
same fuel load [@campbell-lochrie2021], although the relationship is
likely more complex in canopy fuels [@schwilk2015].

-As Andrews [-@andrews2018] noted, Rothermel surface model only
applicable to surface fuels, within \~6ft of the ground

-Thinning increases sub-canopy turbulence [@russell2018]. See also
Banerjee et al. [@banerjee2020]

Method could also be used to estimate influence of dead branch fuel
below LCBH, bark flakes, arboreal lichens, or similar material.

Surface fire ROS model is an important component of all calculations.
Although the SROS models we present are imprecise and gloss over
potentially important differences such as litter type, canopy closure,
overstory species and porosity (as per Rothermel 1972; Campbell-Lochrie
et al. 2021), they are important to show real-world surface fire
conditions in Canadian conifer stands. These models can be used along
with crown fire modelling systems. In addition to the empirical models
shown here, SROS models can be used along with VW77, CanFire, CFIM, or
other tools appropriate to the context and questions of interest.

-What's actually going on in VW77 model: physics probably work (size of
flame under calm conditions, etc.), but SFC isn't instantaneous (only
part of it burns during flaming), and sROS needs to be realistic.

For example, Sharpsand thinned site stands (LCBH=4.44 m) had an ISI
threshold for crowning of 10.3. That ISI value is roughly associated
with ROS (using an aggregated conifer ROS model) of 2.267 m min^-1^. At
a mean SFC of 1.34 kg m^-2^, Byram's (1959) model suggests 911 kW/m
intensity, nowhere near the 1739 kW/m needed for crowning (at mean FMC
of 108 %).

Only problem with this is that, as a calibration study, uses linkages
between several empirical models, all of which appear quite sensitive to
inputs. Thus, sROS models are highly sensitive to small differences in
wind speed and fuel moisture, and the ISI thresholds differences between
IM and TH stands can result in quite different critical sROS values
depending which model is chosen. The SFC and FC.se are also sensitive
and quite variable, with

## References:

::: {#refs}
:::
